In what years did Drake successfully cir: 1572–1575. He became the first Englishman to sail completely around the world, surviving the incredibly dangerous voyage.
1585–1588. He became the first Englishman to sail completely around the world, surviving the incredibly dangerous voyage.
1590–1593. He became the first Englishman to sail completely around the world, surviving the incredibly dangerous voyage.
What was the name of Drake's surviving s: The Tiger. He used it to constantly raid unprotected Spanish colonial ports along the coast of South America.
The Falcon. He used it to constantly raid unprotected Spanish colonial ports along the coast of South America.
The Revenge. He used it to constantly raid unprotected Spanish colonial ports along the coast of South America.
What region in North America did Drake c: Virginia (modern-day North Carolina). He crowned himself in the name of the Queen to establish an English presence in the Americas.
New England (modern-day Massachusetts). He crowned himself in the name of the Queen to establish an English presence in the Americas.
Florida (modern-day Miami). He crowned himself in the name of the Queen to establish an English presence in the Americas.
How did claiming Nova Albion challenge S: It resulted in Spain declaring an immediate trade embargo on all English cloth exports. It signaled that Spain would defend its trade route.
It forced the French King to sign the Treaty of Joinville with Scotland. It signaled that France would protect its trade in Canada.
It allowed Elizabeth to appoint Drake as the royal governor of the Americas. It signaled that England would build a land base in Florida.
Roughly how much stolen Spanish treasure: £50,000. This was an astonishing amount of money that was immediately used to pay off England's national debt.
£100,000. This was an astonishing amount of money that was immediately used to pay off England's national debt.
£1,000,000. This was an astonishing amount of money that was immediately used to pay off England's national debt.
What percentage profit did Drake's priva: Around 100%. This incredible profit spurred a massive boom in English global exploration and private investment.
Around 500%. This incredible profit spurred a massive boom in English global exploration and private investment.
Around 1000%. This incredible profit spurred a massive boom in English global exploration and private investment.
How did Queen Elizabeth reward Drake for: She appointed him as the Lord High Admiral of the Navy. This elevated him from a common sailor to a revered national hero.
She granted him vast estates in the North of England. This elevated him from a common sailor to a revered national hero.
She named him as her chief Secretary of State. This elevated him from a common sailor to a revered national hero.
Why was Drake's knighting highly signifi: It forced Philip II to declare an immediate trade embargo on all English merchants. It proved to Spain that the English government would support the cloth merchants.
It led to the Pope issuing the excommunication bull against Elizabeth. It proved to Spain that the English government had rejected all Catholic treaties.
It convinced France to sign a defensive alliance with England against Spain. It proved to Spain that the English government had formed a naval coalition.
Who legally owned the Netherlands during: France (King Henry III). The Netherlands was an incredibly wealthy province of the French Empire.
England (Queen Elizabeth I). The Netherlands was an incredibly wealthy province of the English Empire.
The Holy Roman Empire (Emperor Rudolph II). The Netherlands was an incredibly wealthy province of the Empire.
Why did the Dutch initially revolt again: They rebelled against the English trade monopoly and high customs duties. The Protestant Dutch demanded economic and religious freedom.
They rebelled against the French military presence and high land rents. The Protestant Dutch demanded economic and religious freedom.
They rebelled against the German Protestant clergy and high church tithes. The Protestant Dutch demanded economic and religious freedom.
Which Dutch Protestant rebel leader was: Maurice of Nassau. His murder left the Dutch rebellion on the verge of collapse, forcing Elizabeth to intervene to stop a total Spanish victory.
Johan van Oldenbarnevelt. His murder left the Dutch rebellion on the verge of collapse, forcing Elizabeth to intervene to stop a total Spanish victory.
Philip William of Orange. His murder left the Dutch rebellion on the verge of collapse, forcing Elizabeth to intervene to stop a total Spanish victory.
What treaty did Elizabeth sign in 1585 t: The Treaty of Nonsuch. This effectively declared war on Spain by officially committing English money and soldiers to fight the Spanish army.
The Treaty of Nonsuch. This effectively declared war on Spain by officially committing English money and soldiers to fight the Spanish army.
The Treaty of Nonsuch. This effectively declared war on Spain by officially committing English money and soldiers to fight the Spanish army.
How many English troops did Elizabeth pr: 3,000 troops. She agreed to finance this army to ensure the Dutch Protestants could hold out against the Duke of Parma.
5,000 troops. She agreed to finance this army to ensure the Dutch Protestants could hold out against the Duke of Parma.
10,000 troops. She agreed to finance this army to ensure the Dutch Protestants could hold out against the Duke of Parma.
Who commanded the English army in the Ne: Robert Devereux, Earl of Essex. He was Elizabeth's favourite courtier but lacked the military genius to defeat the Spanish.
Walter Raleigh, Earl of Devon. He was Elizabeth's favourite courtier but lacked the military genius to defeat the Spanish.
Thomas Sackville, Earl of Dorset. He was Elizabeth's favourite courtier but lacked the military genius to defeat the Spanish.
What title did Dudley accept from the Du: Lord Protector. He accepted this title in 1586 without the Queen's permission.
Stadtholder. He accepted this title in 1586 without the Queen's permission.
High Commissioner. He accepted this title in 1586 without the Queen's permission.
Why did the title 'Governor-General' ang: It implied England was trying to trade with Spanish colonies in the New World. Elizabeth only wanted to protect the rebels, not trade with the Americas.
It implied England was trying to excommunicate King Philip II. Elizabeth only wanted to protect the rebels, not challenge the Pope's authority.
It implied England was trying to force the Dutch to become Anglicans. Elizabeth only wanted to protect the rebels, not change their religion.
Was Dudley's military campaign generally: A success. The campaign was highly funded, the troops were well supplied, and Elizabeth constantly encouraged Dudley to engage in major battles.
A partial success. The campaign managed to capture several major ports, but the troops suffered from lack of food and payment.
A stalemate. The campaign kept the Spanish busy but failed to capture any territory or prevent Spanish raids.
Which vital deep-water port did Dudley s: Antwerp. Holding this port was critical because it meant the Spanish Armada later had no safe, deep harbor to dock in to load troops.
Flushing. Holding this port was critical because it meant the Spanish Armada later had no safe, deep harbor to dock in to load troops.
Brill. Holding this port was critical because it meant the Spanish Armada later had no safe, deep harbor to dock in to load troops.
In what month and year did Drake attack: August 1585. Elizabeth sent him to disrupt the Spanish fleet as they were actively building the Armada.
October 1586. Elizabeth sent him to disrupt the Spanish fleet as they were actively building the Armada.
June 1588. Elizabeth sent him to disrupt the Spanish fleet as they were actively building the Armada.
What was Cadiz?: A heavily fortified Spanish colony in Flanders. It was the main gathering point for Philip II's invasion fleet.
A heavily fortified Spanish military academy in Madrid. It was the main gathering point for Philip II's invasion fleet.
A heavily fortified Spanish trading post in the Caribbean. It was the main gathering point for Philip II's invasion fleet.
What famous nickname was given to Drake': Slapping the King of Spain's face. This reflected how Drake humiliated the most powerful king in the world right on his own doorstep.
Stealing the King of Spain's crown. This reflected how Drake humiliated the most powerful king in the world right on his own doorstep.
Burning the King of Spain's fleet. This reflected how Drake humiliated the most powerful king in the world right on his own doorstep.
Roughly how many Spanish ships did Drake: Around 10 ships. He launched a daring surprise attack, burning and sinking the vessels before the Spanish could respond.
Around 50 ships. He launched a daring surprise attack, burning and sinking the vessels before the Spanish could respond.
Around 100 ships. He launched a daring surprise attack, burning and sinking the vessels before the Spanish could respond.
What vital wooden items did Drake destro: Oars. These were essential for powering the slow, heavy Spanish troop ships.
Planks. These were essential for repairing damaged hulls of the Spanish Armada.
Masts. These were essential for supporting sails on the Spanish galleons.
Why were the barrel staves so important: They were needed to construct the defensive barriers on the decks of the ships. Without them, soldiers were exposed to enemy fire.
They were needed to build the small transport barges to load troops. Without them, Medina Sidonia could not reach the shore.
They were needed to build secure storage boxes for the gunpowder. Without them, ammunition could easily get wet and useless.
What happened to the Spanish food during: It had to be left behind in Spain, causing the soldiers to go hungry during the entire voyage.
It was seized by English privateers in the Channel, causing the soldiers to go hungry during the retreat.
It had to be thrown overboard during the storms, causing the soldiers to go hungry during the retreat.
After leaving Cadiz, what did Drake do o: He blockaded the port of Lisbon to prevent the Armada from sailing, but was forced to retreat when Spanish reinforcements arrived.
He landed troops to launch a ground attack on Madrid, but was forced to retreat when Spanish reinforcements arrived.
He signed a secret trade agreement with Portuguese merchants, but was forced to cancel it when the Spanish King discovered the plot.
How long did Drake's raid on Cadiz delay: By a few weeks. The destruction of ships and supplies forced Philip II to halt the invasion until 1588.
By six months. The destruction of ships and supplies forced Philip II to halt the invasion until 1588.
By two years. The destruction of ships and supplies forced Philip II to halt the invasion until 1588.
How long did this delay buy for England?: It bought Elizabeth crucial time to sign a defensive alliance with France and the Netherlands.
It bought Elizabeth crucial time to raise land rents and collect taxes to fund her army.
It bought Elizabeth crucial time to negotiate a peace treaty with the Pope and Spain.
In what year did the Spanish Armada fina: 1585. The massive fleet set sail from Lisbon, Portugal, towards the English Channel.
1586. The massive fleet set sail from Lisbon, Portugal, towards the English Channel.
1587. The massive fleet set sail from Lisbon, Portugal, towards the English Channel.
How many ships made up the Spanish Armad: 50 ships. It was the largest invasion fleet Europe had ever seen, carrying roughly 30,000 sailors and soldiers.
100 ships. It was the largest invasion fleet Europe had ever seen, carrying roughly 30,000 sailors and soldiers.
200 ships. It was the largest invasion fleet Europe had ever seen, carrying roughly 30,000 sailors and soldiers.
Who chose Medina Sidonia to command the: The Pope chosen Medina Sidonia to command the Armada. He was an incredibly wealthy noble chosen for his rank, rather than his military skill.
The Duke of Parma chosen Medina Sidonia to command the Armada. He was an incredibly wealthy noble chosen for his rank, rather than his military skill.
The Spanish Council chosen Medina Sidonia to command the Armada. He was an incredibly wealthy noble chosen for his rank, rather than his military skill.
Why was the Duke of Medina Sidonia a poo: He was too old and could not physically withstand the strain of a long voyage. He actually begged the King not to send him.
He was a Protestant sympathizer who secretly wanted England to win the war. He actually begged the King not to send him.
He was a soldier who preferred land warfare and refused to board the ship. He actually begged the King not to send him.
What was the Armada's primary strategic: To launch a surprise attack on London from the Thames. This would allow the Spanish to capture the Queen and declare victory.
To blockade the English Channel and starve the country into surrender. This would allow the Spanish to dictate terms of peace.
To land troops in Ireland and launch a joint invasion with Irish Catholics. This would allow the Spanish to march on London from the West.
What defensive shape did the Spanish fle: A straight line formation. This allowed the faster galleons to lead the attack and fire their cannons at the English fleet.
A circle formation. This allowed the ships to defend themselves from all sides against English privateer raids.
A wedge formation. This allowed the heavy transports to smash through the English defensive lines in the Channel.
Where did the Spanish Armada drop anchor: Dover. They stopped here to wait for messages from the Duke of Parma.
Gravelines. They stopped here to wait for messages from the Duke of Parma.
Ostend. They stopped here to wait for messages from the Duke of Parma.
What tactic did the English use to break: They used long-range cannons to sink the slower supply ships. This forced the Spanish to scatter and retreat.
They launched a pre-emptive board attack on the flagship San Martin. This forced the Spanish to scatter and retreat.
They simulated a retreat to draw the Spanish galleons away. This forced the Spanish to scatter and retreat.
What decisive naval battle took place on: The Battle of Cadiz. The English attacked the scattered Spanish ships, dealing heavy damage and preventing them from linking up with Parma.
The Battle of Calais. The English attacked the scattered Spanish ships, dealing heavy damage and preventing them from linking up with Parma.
The Battle of Dover. The English attacked the scattered Spanish ships, dealing heavy damage and preventing them from linking up with Parma.
What route was the Armada forced to take: South, directly through the English Channel under the cover of night. The wind direction and the English fleet blocked their return.
West, across the open Atlantic towards the Azores. The wind direction and the English fleet blocked their return.
East, through the Baltic Sea and around Scandinavia. The wind direction and the English fleet blocked their return.
What type of galleons did the English us: Caravels. These had lower castles and sleeker hulls, making them much faster than the bulky Spanish troop transports.
Carracks. These had lower castles and sleeker hulls, making them much faster than the bulky Spanish troop transports.
Galleasses. These had lower castles and sleeker hulls, making them much faster than the bulky Spanish troop transports.
What advantage did English cannons (culv: They had a longer range and could fire heavier iron balls. This allowed the English to batter the Spanish ships from safety.
They were mounted on swivel stands and could be aimed in any direction. This allowed the English to fire at any angle.
They used advanced gunpowder that did not create smoke. This allowed the English to see the enemy clearly during battle.
What tactic did the English use at Grave: They launched fireships into the scattered fleet during the day, preventing Medina Sidonia from reloading his guns.
They blockaded the harbor to trap the Spanish ships inside, preventing them from engaging in naval maneuvers.
They used speed to launch quick hit-and-run attacks on the slower transports, avoiding the heavy galleons completely.
How did poor communication ruin the Span: It took a week for messages to reach the King in Spain, meaning Medina Sidonia did not have permission to attack the English fleet.
It took a week for messages to reach the French allies, meaning the Duke of Guise did not bring his fleet to assist.
It took a week for messages to reach the Pope in Rome, meaning the Armada did not receive the holy blessing before the battle.
Why couldn't the Duke of Parma's troops: Because the English navy had blockaded the Dutch ports, preventing the barges from leaving the canals.
Because the Dutch rebels had launched a land attack on Parma's camp, forcing him to defend his base.
Because the Spanish soldiers refused to board the small barges, demanding larger warships for safety.
How did Drake's 1587 raid contribute to: The destruction of the timber forests in Spain meant Medina Sidonia had to build ships with inferior wood, causing them to leak.
The destruction of the gold shipment meant Spain could not pay the soldiers, causing a mutiny before the launch.
The destruction of the naval academy meant Spain had no trained captains, causing confusion during the naval maneuvers.
What role did the weather play in the to: Extreme heat caused the gunpowder to explode in the hot holds of the Spanish galleons, sinking several ships.
Heavy fog in the Channel caused the Spanish ships to collide with each other in the dark, sinking several ships.
Lack of wind in the Channel left the Spanish ships sitting ducks for English cannon fire, sinking several ships.
What term was used by English propaganda: The 'Wind of God'. The English claimed that God had sent the storms to deliberately protect Protestantism and destroy the Catholics.
The 'Divine Breeze'. The English claimed that God had sent the storms to deliberately protect Protestantism and destroy the Catholics.
The 'Anglican Storm'. The English claimed that God had sent the storms to deliberately protect Protestantism and destroy the Catholics.
Out of the original 130, how many Spanis: Only 30 ships. Thousands of Spanish sailors died of disease, starvation, or drowned during the retreat.
Only 50 ships. Thousands of Spanish sailors died of disease, starvation, or drowned during the retreat.
Only 100 ships. Thousands of Spanish sailors died of disease, starvation, or drowned during the retreat.
How did the victory impact Elizabeth's i: It convinced Parliament to name her as the absolute ruler of England, giving her the power to govern without consulting them.
It convinced the Pope to lift the excommunication bull and recognize her as the rightful Queen of England.
It convinced France to sign a trade treaty that lowered taxes on English cloth, boosting the economy.
Name the philosophical movement that hea: Scholasticism. Humanists believed that education was essential to fulfil human potential and create a civilised society, not just for training priests.
Rationalism. Humanists believed that education was essential to fulfil human potential and create a civilised society, not just for training priests.
Empiricism. Humanists believed that education was essential to fulfil human potential and create a civilised society, not just for training priests.
What type of school was founded for the: Petty schools. During Elizabeth's reign, 72 new fee-paying grammar schools were opened across the country.
Dame schools. During Elizabeth's reign, 72 new fee-paying grammar schools were opened across the country.
Public schools. During Elizabeth's reign, 72 new fee-paying grammar schools were opened across the country.
What two classical subjects were heavily: Mathematics and geometry. These were considered the language of the educated elite and were essential for anyone wanting to study law or medicine.
History and geography. These were considered the language of the educated elite and were essential for anyone wanting to study law or medicine.
Theology and scripture. These were considered the language of the educated elite and were essential for anyone wanting to study law or medicine.
What were 'Petty Schools'?: Fee-paying schools run by the clergy. They taught advanced Latin, Greek, and theology to prepare boys for university.
Home schools run by the gentry. They taught social skills, languages, and fencing to prepare noble children for court.
Free schools run by local town guilds. They taught trade skills and merchant accounting to prepare boys for apprenticeships.
What were 'Dame Schools'?: Advanced boarding schools for noble girls. They taught foreign languages, music, and embroidery to prepare them for marriage.
Catholic schools run by nuns in secret. They taught Latin liturgy and catechism to Catholic children in the North.
Special schools for training female servants. They taught cooking, cleaning, and sewing to prepare girls for work.
How were the children of the wealthy nob: By attending prestigious grammar schools in London, such as St. Paul's. They lived in boarding houses and studied classical subjects.
By attending universities at Oxford and Cambridge from a very young age. They lived in college quarters and studied law.
By attending parish schools run by the local Protestant bishop. They studied theology and bible readings alongside local children.
Name the only two universities in Elizab: London and Oxford. They expanded during this period to train the clergy and professional classes in subjects like geometry, astronomy, and rhetoric.
Cambridge and London. They expanded during this period to train the clergy and professional classes in subjects like geometry, astronomy, and rhetoric.
Oxford and Bristol. They expanded during this period to train the clergy and professional classes in subjects like geometry, astronomy, and rhetoric.
What was the approximate male literacy r: Around 10%. This significant improvement was driven by the new grammar schools and the practical need for merchants to read contracts.
Around 50%. This significant improvement was driven by the new grammar schools and the practical need for merchants to read contracts.
Around 70%. This significant improvement was driven by the new grammar schools and the practical need for merchants to read contracts.
What was the approximate female literacy: Around 1%. Female literacy remained very low because society believed girls did not need a formal education and should focus on domestic skills.
Around 5%. Female literacy remained very low because society believed girls did not need a formal education and should focus on domestic skills.
Around 20%. Female literacy remained very low because society believed girls did not need a formal education and should focus on domestic skills.
How did the Protestant religion encourag: Protestants believed that the Church should run all grammar schools, motivating parishes to raise funds for new school buildings.
Protestants believed that only educated men could understand the sermons, motivating parishes to teach advanced Latin.
Protestants believed that learning classical subjects would help fight Catholic arguments, motivating parishes to teach philosophy.
Name a sport played almost exclusively b: Mob football. These sports required expensive equipment, horses, or purpose-built indoor courts that only the rich could afford.
Bear-baiting. These sports required expensive equipment, horses, or purpose-built indoor courts that only the rich could afford.
Archery. These sports required expensive equipment, horses, or purpose-built indoor courts that only the rich could afford.
What was 'mob football'?: A game similar to tennis played on grass courts by the gentry. It was a rule-based game that focused on physical agility and skill.
A game similar to cricket played in public parks by the merchant class. It was a rule-based game that focused on batting and fielding.
A game similar to rugby played in schoolyards by grammar school boys. It was a rule-based game that focused on running and passing.
Name two popular 'cruel sports' watched: Mob football and bear-baiting. People from all backgrounds would mix to watch these violent animal fights and bet large sums of money on the outcome.
Archery and cock-fighting. People from all backgrounds would mix to watch these violent animal fights and bet large sums of money on the outcome.
Real tennis and bear-baiting. People from all backgrounds would mix to watch these violent animal fights and bet large sums of money on the outcome.
What was a 'bear garden'?: A royal park in London where the Queen kept her private collection of bears. It was used for royal guests to walk and view the animals.
A local marketplace where trained bears performed tricks and danced for crowds. It was a popular form of street entertainment.
A specialized cage in the zoo where bears were bred for export to European courts. It was a profitable trade business in London.
What change occurred in the type of play: Traditional secular comedies were replaced by religious mystery plays detailing stories from the Bible to educate the public.
Traditional court performances in Latin were replaced by popular street theater in English to entertain the lower classes.
Traditional musical performances were replaced by complex poetry recitals written by court poets to show off their humanist education.
Name one of the first purpose-built thea: The Globe, The Rose, or The Fortune.
The Curtain, The Rose, or The Fortune.
The Swan, The Rose, or The Fortune.
How much did the poor pay to stand in th: Two pennies. These audience members, known as 'groundlings', made the theatre an incredibly popular and accessible form of mass entertainment.
Three pennies. These audience members, known as 'groundlings', made the theatre an incredibly popular and accessible form of mass entertainment.
A halfpenny. These audience members, known as 'groundlings', made the theatre an incredibly popular and accessible form of mass entertainment.
Where did the wealthy sit in the theatre: In the front rows of the pit. They paid more money for comfortable seating, protection from the weather, and to show off their high social status.
On the stage itself alongside the actors. They paid more money for comfortable seating, protection from the weather, and to show off their high social status.
In private boxes behind the stage. They paid more money for comfortable seating, protection from the weather, and to show off their high social status.
Why did acting troupes need the patronag: To raise the massive funds needed to build theatres in London. Without the backing of a noble, they could not afford the building licenses.
To write the scripts and design the costumes for the performances. Without the backing of a noble, they lacked the education and style.
To avoid paying taxes on their ticket sales to the city officials. Without the backing of a noble, they were treated as illegal businesses.
Which strict religious group heavily opp: Moderate Catholics. They believed theatres were the work of the devil, encouraged sinful behaviour, and distracted people from prayer.
High Church Anglicans. They believed theatres were the work of the devil, encouraged sinful behaviour, and distracted people from prayer.
Humanist scholars. They believed theatres were the work of the devil, encouraged sinful behaviour, and distracted people from prayer.
What happened to the population of Engla: It grew slowly from 3 million in 1551 to 3.5 million by 1601. This demographical growth created moderate pressure on food and jobs.
It grew rapidly from 2 million in 1551 to 4 million by 1601. This demographic boom created massive pressure on food and jobs.
It grew rapidly from 4 million in 1551 to 6 million by 1601. This demographic boom created massive pressure on food and jobs.
How did this rapid population growth aff: It drove wages up. Because there was a massive shortage of available workers, employers had to pay more to attract labor.
It had no effect on wages. Because wages were strictly regulated by the Justices of the Peace in each county.
It caused wages to fluctuate wildly. Because the cloth trade was constantly disrupted by the war with Spain.
What agricultural practice involved fenc: Enclosure. This allowed peasants to buy their own fields and farm them efficiently, improving the agricultural economy.
Enclosure. This was a system of crop rotation that increased the harvest yield and reduced food prices in the country.
Enclosure. This was a method of draining marshes to create new fertile fields for growing wheat and barley.
Why did many landowners switch from grow: The government offered financial grants for sheep farming. Landowners hoped to receive these subsidies to pay off their land debts.
Sheep farming was easier to manage than crop farming. Landowners wanted to reduce the number of workers on their estates for safety.
The demand for meat was growing rapidly in the towns. Landowners wanted to supply the butcher guilds for high profits.
How did the shift to sheep farming direc: Sheep farming required more physical labour than crop farming. This created a labor shortage that landowners could not afford to pay.
Sheep farming led to a decline in the fertility of the soil. This meant that landowners could not grow crops and had to lay off workers.
Sheep farming caused conflicts between landowners and shepherds over wages. This led to a series of local strikes and job dismissals.
What economic problem severely reduced t: Taxes. The Queen levied high customs duties and poll taxes to pay for her wars in the Netherlands and defend against the Armada.
Devaluation. The international merchants refused to accept English coins because they believed the currency was unstable.
Interest rates. The Antwerp Exchange charged high interest rates for royal loans, reducing the money available in England.
What natural disasters caused food price: A series of disastrous bad harvests, particularly in 1570 and 1580.
A series of disastrous bad harvests, particularly in 1575 and 1585.
A series of disastrous bad harvests, particularly in 1588 and 1598.
What traditional safety net for the poor: The guild halls. Before their dissolution, guilds had historically provided charity, food, and shelter for unemployed workers.
The parish system. Before their dissolution, parishes had historically collected poor rates to support vulnerable families.
The royal charities. Before their dissolution, the Crown had distributed free bread and silver to poor citizens in London.
Who were the 'impotent' (or deserving) p: Homeless beggars who refused the offer of work. Elizabethan society believed it was their duty to punish this group as lazy criminals.
Unemployed farm laborers who traveled looking for work. Elizabethan society believed it was their duty to find them jobs in the parish.
Protestant refugees fleeing religious wars in Europe. Elizabethan society believed it was their duty to provide them shelter and charity.
Who were the 'idle' (or able-bodied) poo: Genuinely unable to work due to age, illness, or disability. Elizabethan society believed it was their duty to support this group.
Unemployed merchants who lost their business in Antwerp. Elizabethan society believed it was their duty to give them financial grants.
Protestant priests who were sacked for refusing to take the oath. Elizabethan society believed it was their duty to help them.
What was a 'vagabond' (or vagrant)?: A criminal who committed theft or assault in the towns. They were treated as high-risk convicts and faced immediate execution.
A merchant who traded illegally in the New World without a licence. They were treated as pirates and faced arrest by Spain.
A Protestant priest who preached without a license in the parish. They were treated as heretics and faced dismissal by the bishop.
Give two reasons why the Elizabethan gov: They believed vagabonds would form a private army to support a Spanish invasion and help the Pope restore Catholicism.
They believed vagabonds would raid the royal palaces, steal the crown treasures, and kidnap the Queen for ransom.
They believed vagabonds would establish secret Protestant sects, challenge the bishops, and destroy the Religious Settlement.
What penalty was introduced for local of: A fine of up to £5. This showed the government was starting to force local authorities to take the poverty crisis seriously.
A fine of up to £10. This showed the government was starting to force local authorities to take the poverty crisis seriously.
A fine of up to £50. This showed the government was starting to force local authorities to take the poverty crisis seriously.
What harsh physical punishments did the: Begging vagrants were to be executed on the first offense. This brutal retaliation was designed to crush poverty completely.
Begging vagrants were to be branded on the forehead with the letter 'V' and sent to the galleys as oarsmen for the navy.
Begging vagrants were to be fined five shillings and banned from entering any towns in the county.
Despite its brutality, what helpful syst: A system of houses of correction to provide shelter and basic training for the able-bodied poor in the parish.
A system of raw materials distribution to help the unemployed make goods to sell and earn a living.
A system of local gentry committees to investigate the causes of poverty and recommend economic reforms.
What was the role of the local 'Overseer: To arrest vagrants, hold trials in the churchyard, and carry out the whippings or ear drilling punishments.
To manage the house of correction, supervise the work of the able-bodied poor, and sell the goods they produced.
To inspect local grammar schools, verify the licenses of teachers, and report on religious education.
What was the punishment for a citizen wh: They were banned from attending the parish church and faced recusancy fines. The government made poor relief a mandatory duty.
They were forced to house vagrants in their homes for a month. The government made poor relief a mandatory civic duty.
They were fined £5 and had their property seized by the Overseer. The government made poor relief a mandatory civic duty.
What were Justices of the Peace (JPs) re: Establish petty schools in every parish to teach poor children reading and writing. This helped them find apprenticeships.
Build houses of correction in every county to punish able-bodied poor who refused to work. This helped maintain order.
Collect poor rates from all residents in the county and distribute the money to the impotent poor. This helped support the vulnerable.
What was a 'House of Correction'?: A free school for poor children run by the town council. They learned trade skills and basic literacy to prepare for work.
A local hospital for the impotent poor run by the Church. They received free medical care and shelter until they recovered.
A shelter for homeless vagrants run by the Overseer. They received food and clean clothing in exchange for cleaning the streets.
How did government attitudes towards pov: They shifted from helping the deserving poor to treating all beggars as lazy criminals who should be punished and deported.
They shifted from organizing local charity to forcing all poor people to emigrate to the new colony in Virginia.
They shifted from blaming landowners for enclosing common land to blaming the merchant class for rising prices.
Why did English merchants desperately ne: Because the French King had raised customs duties on English cloth, making it unprofitable to sell in Paris.
Because the Dutch rebels had blockaded the Channel ports, making it impossible to transport wool to Antwerp.
Because the Pope had banned Catholic merchants from buying Protestant goods, ruining the trade in Italy.
Name two highly demanded luxury goods th: Wheat, timber, iron, and copper. Finding direct trade routes to these goods would make merchants incredibly rich.
Cotton, coffee, tea, and porcelain. Finding direct trade routes to these goods would make merchants incredibly rich.
Wool, linen, leather, and tallow. Finding direct trade routes to these goods would make merchants incredibly rich.
Who established the brutal 'triangular t: Francis Drake. He took iron goods to Africa to buy enslaved people, sold them in the New World, and brought sugar and pearls back to England.
Walter Raleigh. He took iron goods to Africa to buy enslaved people, sold them in the New World, and brought sugar and pearls back to England.
Richard Grenville. He took iron goods to Africa to buy enslaved people, sold them in the New World, and brought sugar and pearls back to England.
What new type of ship was developed by t: The caravel. These ships were larger, much more stable in heavy seas, and carried heavy cannons for defence against pirates.
The carrack. These ships were larger, much more stable in heavy seas, and carried heavy cannons for defence against pirates.
The dromon. These ships were larger, much more stable in heavy seas, and carried heavy cannons for defence against pirates.
What specific type of sail made galleons: Square sails. This allowed the ships to catch the wind more efficiently and sail faster.
Lug sails. This allowed the ships to catch the wind more efficiently and sail faster.
Sprit sails. This allowed the ships to catch the wind more efficiently and sail faster.
Name two new navigational instruments th: The compass and the sundial. These allowed captains to accurately determine their latitude using the position of the stars.
The sextant and the chronometer. These allowed captains to accurately determine their latitude using the position of the stars.
The telescope and the barometer. These allowed captains to accurately determine their latitude using the position of the stars.
What invention allowed accurate navigati: The steam engine. This meant details of safe trade routes and geography could be shared among English sailors.
The paper mill. This meant details of safe trade routes and geography could be shared among English sailors.
The typewriter. This meant details of safe trade routes and geography could be shared among English sailors.
What vital map, created in 1569, helped: The Ptolemy Map. It used parallel lines of longitude and latitude to give sailors a highly realistic, standardized picture of the world.
The Ortelius Map. It used parallel lines of longitude and latitude to give sailors a highly realistic, standardized picture of the world.
The Waldseemüller Map. It used parallel lines of longitude and latitude to give sailors a highly realistic, standardized picture of the world.
How were incredibly expensive explorator: Through direct royal taxes levied by the Queen. Parliament passed special exploratory taxes to fund the ships and supplies.
Through financial loans from the Antwerp Exchange. The Queen borrowed gold from Italian bankers, using Crown lands as security.
Through charitable donations from the Church of England. Bishops organized parish collections to support the exploration.
Why did English privateers specifically: Because the native people had indicated they wanted to trade cloth for spices. Privateers wanted to build trading posts.
Because they wanted to find a safe route to trade with Portuguese colonies in Brazil. Privateers wanted to avoid conflicts.
Because they wanted to establish Protestant churches in the Americas. Privateers wanted to spread the Reformation.
